content.problemStatement:
There is a party going on in a club situated in some city. **N** gangsters will attend that party. The **i**-th gangster comes to the party at the beginning of **Si** time and will leave the party at the end of **Ei**. At any time unit, the set of gangsters who are present in the party enjoy drinking together and become friends. After the party, the law enforcement agency in the city is suspecting that there has been some conspiracy for sabotage in the city. So they feel that it is necessary to interrogate several gangsters. But they don't have time to interrogate all of them. They are suspecting that there are **X** gangsters involved in this conspiracy. It is natural that those **X** gangsters will be friends with each other.

Now the enforcement agency have got the idea. They want to interrogate the gangsters in such a way that for each subset of **X** friend gangsters (where each pair of gangsters in that subset are friends), there will be at least one interrogated gangster from that subset. But to save their time, they want to interrogate minimum number of gangsters. Given the information of the gangsters and number **X**, help them to find the minimum number of gangsters to be interrogated.

**Note**: Two gangsters are friends if they have common time in the club. For example, the gangster entering at time 1 and leaving at time 3 can be a friend with another gangster entering at time 3 and leaving some time afterwards. But a gangster leaving at time 3 cannot be a friend with another gangster entering at time 4.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and X, where N is the number of gangsters and X is the number of gangsters suspected to be involved in the conspiracy. The second line contains N pairs of integers, each representing the start and end time of a gangster.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print the minimum number of gangsters that need to be interrogated.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, gangsters 1 and 2 are friends, and gangsters 2 and 3 are friends. So, it is enough to interrogate gangster 2 only. For the second test case, all four gangsters are friends with each other. The police suspect that the size of the group is 3, so it is enough to interrogate any two of the gangsters. For the third test case, there is no group of 3 gangsters where each of them are friends with each other, so the police don't need to interrogate anybody.

The police suspect that the size of the group is 3. The only group of 3 gangsters where each of them are friends with each other is {1, 2, 3}. So, it is enough to interrogate any two of the gangsters.
The police suspect that the size of the group is 2. All four gangsters are friends with each other. So, it is enough to interrogate any one of the gangsters.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved by constructing a graph where each gangster is a node, and two nodes are connected if the corresponding gangsters are friends. Then, we need to find the minimum vertex cover of the graph, which is the minimum number of nodes that need to be selected such that every edge in the graph is incident to at least one of the selected nodes. This can be done using bit manipulation to generate all possible subsets of nodes and checking if each subset is a vertex cover.
solution.timeComplexity: O(2^N * N^2) per test case, O(T * 2^N * N^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2) — for the graph adjacency matrix.
